network.overall_strategy: This network models the prebiotic synthesis of L-threonine through two major experimentally supported frameworks: (1) the Sutherland cyanosulfidic protometabolism, where UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN yields glycolaldehyde and glyceraldehyde, whose triose chemistry inevitably produces the threonine aminonitrile precursor (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile), and (2) the prebiotic sugar pathway, where aldol condensation of C1-C3 aldehydes produces tetrose sugars (erythrose) that react with NH₃ and H₂S to form activated threonine thioesters. Hydrothermal environments generate the foundational C1-C2 building blocks (CO, formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, HCN) via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis and volcanic outgassing on greigite and magnetite catalysts. Surface environments drive the critical cyanosulfidic photochemistry with Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling under UV irradiation, formose-type sugar assembly on borate minerals, Strecker synthesis on montmorillonite, and the prebiotic sugar pathway with thiol activation. Biochemical assembly finalizes hydrolysis of aminonitriles and thioesters to free threonine. Four hub molecules—HCN, formaldehyde, glycolaldehyde, and glyceraldehyde—create a highly interconnected network with multiple redundant routes, ensuring robust synthesis under variable early-Earth conditions.

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: Alkaline hydrothermal vent, pH 9-11, serpentinization-derived H₂

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ binds to the Fe₃S₄ surface and is reduced by H₂ via a two-electron transfer to yield formate (HCOO⁻). The reaction is analogous to the first step of the biological Wood-Ljungdahl pathway, where greigite acts as a structural analog of biological [Fe₄S₄] clusters.
reactions.reasoning: Greigite-catalyzed CO₂ reduction to formate is one of the best-supported hydrothermal prebiotic reactions. The structural analogy between greigite and biological iron-sulfur clusters supports the metabolism-first hypothesis (Russell & Hall, 1997; Preiner et al., 2020). The literature review confirms that transition metal sulfide minerals are central to the metabolism-first hypothesis and catalyze CO and CO₂ fixation via pathways prefiguring the Wood-Ljungdahl pathway.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift on magnetite

conditions.other: Black smoker or subseafloor hydrothermal system

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ is reduced to CO via the reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite surfaces. The reaction proceeds through a surface-bound carboxylate intermediate that loses an oxygen atom to yield CO and H₂O.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction on magnetite is well-documented in Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry under hydrothermal conditions (McCollom & Seewald, 2007). CO is an essential C₁ building block that feeds into formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and HCN synthesis. The literature notes that Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite produces hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aldehydes at 400-500K.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of formaldehyde
reactions.mechanism: CO adsorbs onto the magnetite surface and undergoes stepwise hydrogenation. The first hydrogenation yields a surface-bound formyl intermediate (HCO*), which is further hydrogenated to formaldehyde (H₂CO). Rapid removal from the catalytic surface into cooler surrounding water prevents over-reduction to methanol.
reactions.reasoning: Fischer-Tropsch-type (FTT) synthesis of formaldehyde from CO and H₂ on magnetite surfaces is a classical result in hydrothermal prebiotic chemistry (McCollom et al., 1999; Holm & Hennet, 1992). Formaldehyde is a critical C₁ hub molecule that feeds into the formose reaction and aldol condensation pathways. The research confirms formaldehyde as a key precursor to glycolaldehyde and sugar synthesis.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: FTT product distribution favors C₁-C₃ oxygenates

reactions.mechanism: CO undergoes CO-insertion and hydrogenation on the magnetite surface to form a surface-bound acetyl intermediate, which is hydrogenated to acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO). This is a classical FTT product alongside methanol and formaldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Acetaldehyde is a well-known product of Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry on iron oxide catalysts (McCollom & Seewald, 2007). It serves as a C₂ building block for cross-aldol condensation to erythrose. The literature specifically notes that parent body processing of formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and ammonia during melting of parent body ice produces amino acid precursors, supporting extraterrestrial formation of threonine precursors (Pizzarello, 2004; Ehrenfreund et al., 2001).

reactions.name: Volcanic HCN synthesis from NH₃ and CO

conditions.other: Volcanic vent gas-phase reaction, analogous to lightning-driven HCN production; FeS₂ surface catalysis at high T

reactions.mechanism: NH₃ and CO react on pyrite surfaces at high temperatures through a formamide intermediate. Dehydration of formamide yields HCN. This reaction mirrors volcanic gas-phase chemistry where HCN is produced from N₂/NH₃ and carbon sources at high energy.
reactions.reasoning: HCN is a cornerstone feedstock for the cyanosulfidic protometabolism. Volcanic and hydrothermal production of HCN from nitrogen and carbon sources is well-supported (Tian et al., 2011; Rimmer & Rugheimer, 2019). Sutherland's cyanosulfidic framework explicitly requires HCN as the primary one-carbon nitrogen source from which all amino acid and nucleotide precursors are built (Patel et al., 2015). Pyrite catalysis of gas-phase reactions at high temperature is documented (Wächtershäuser, 1988).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic reductive homologation of HCN to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation (λ < 300 nm, no ozone layer), shallow evaporitic pool, H₂S as stoichiometric reductant, periodic drying concentrates HCN

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes UV-driven reductive homologation. Cu(I) coordinates with cyanide to form a Cu-CN complex. UV photoexcitation drives single-electron transfer from H₂S to Cu(II), regenerating Cu(I) and producing HS• radicals. The activated HCN dimerizes via radical coupling to form glycolonitrile, which is hydrolyzed to glycolaldehyde. TiO₂ anatase surfaces enhance UV absorption and radical generation.
reactions.reasoning: This is the central reaction of Sutherland's cyanosulfidic protometabolism (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012). The literature explicitly states that UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN, driven by H₂S as reductant, produces glycolaldehyde (C₂) as a primary product. Cu(I)-Cu(II) photoredox cycling accelerates carbon-carbon bond formation and stabilizes reactive intermediates. The presence of copper is described as 'essential' in the research findings.

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic chain extension to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: UV irradiation, continuation of cyanosulfidic cascade, shallow pool or stream chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde undergoes cyanohydrin formation with HCN to produce glycolonitrile-aldehyde, which is then reduced by H₂S under UV and Cu-mediated photoredox to form glyceraldehyde. The Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycle provides the reducing equivalents for the two-electron reduction of the nitrile to the aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The chain extension from glycolaldehyde (C₂) to glyceraldehyde (C₃) is the second step in Sutherland's reductive homologation cascade (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012). The literature explicitly notes that HCN homologation produces glycolaldehyde and then glyceraldehyde as sequential products. The flow-chemistry model spatially separates these steps along geochemical gradients.

reactions.name: Formose reaction: formaldehyde self-condensation to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: Alkaline evaporitic pool, pH 10-12, borate stabilizes sugar intermediates and prevents tar formation; wet-dry cycling concentrates formaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of formaldehyde undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation (the first step of the formose reaction). Ca(OH)₂ provides the alkaline conditions, and the initial step produces glycolaldehyde via a Cannizzaro-type disproportionation followed by aldol addition. Borate minerals complex with the cis-diol intermediates, stabilizing the product and preventing runaway polymerization to tar.
reactions.reasoning: The formose reaction is a classical prebiotic route from formaldehyde to sugars (Butlerov, 1861; Ricardo et al., 2004). Borate stabilization of sugar intermediates was demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004). The literature confirms that formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde are the foundational C₁ and C₂ feedstocks for the prebiotic sugar pathway. Montmorillonite concentrates organic reactants in interlayer spaces, protecting intermediates from hydrolytic degradation.

reactions.name: Glyceraldehyde isomerization to dihydroxyacetone

conditions.other: 59% yield observed; thermodynamic product; clay surface facilitates keto-enol tautomerism

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes a 1,2-hydride shift (Lobry de Bruyn–Alberda van Ekenstein transformation) to form dihydroxyacetone, the more thermodynamically stable C₃ isomer. The montmorillonite surface provides Brønsted acid sites that catalyze the enolization step.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly reports a 59% yield for the conversion of glyceraldehyde to dihydroxyacetone in simulated cyanosulfidic environments (Patel et al., 2015). Dihydroxyacetone is a key branch point: it participates in subsequent sugar assembly and can also undergo aldol condensation to form tetrose sugars. Montmorillonite is well-documented to catalyze sugar isomerization reactions.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde to erythrose

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite with NH₃ catalysis at pH ~5.2
conditions.other: Evaporitic pool; borate complexation stabilizes erythrose product; wet-dry cycling drives condensation; ammonia or existing amino acids catalyze at moderate pH

reactions.mechanism: Two molecules of glycolaldehyde undergo aldol condensation. The enolizable glycolaldehyde forms an enolate under mildly basic conditions (NH₃ catalysis at pH 5.2), which attacks the carbonyl of a second glycolaldehyde molecule to form erythrose (a C₄ aldotetrose). Borate complexes with the cis-1,2-diol of erythrose, stabilizing it against retro-aldol cleavage.
reactions.reasoning: Aldol condensation of glycolaldehyde to form tetrose sugars (erythrose, threose) is a key step in the prebiotic sugar pathway described in the literature. The research explicitly states that 'aldol condensation between formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde generates tetrose sugars, such as erythrose, threose, and erythrulose' and that 'ammonia or existing amino acids can catalyze these steps at moderate pH (e.g., pH 5.2)'. Self-condensation of glycolaldehyde is equally plausible and has been demonstrated experimentally (Weber, 2001; Kim et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Strecker-type synthesis of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite interlayer surfaces for reactant concentration; Cu catalysis for selectivity
conditions.other: UV irradiation enhances imine formation; clay interlayer concentrates HCN and NH₃; partial stereoselectivity from mineral surface chirality

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form an α-hydroxy imine (Schiff base). HCN then attacks the electrophilic carbon of the imine via nucleophilic addition to yield 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile (the threonine aminonitrile). The montmorillonite interlayer provides a confined reaction space that concentrates the three reactants and may impose partial diastereoselectivity via surface templating. The Cu(I)/Cu(II) system facilitates the overall cyanosulfidic cascade.
reactions.reasoning: The Sutherland group identifies the threonine aminonitrile (2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile, compound 16 in their nomenclature) as an 'inevitable byproduct' of the same triose chemistry that yields glyceraldehyde and dihydroxyacetone (Patel et al., 2015; Sutherland, 2017). The Strecker reaction of α-hydroxy aldehydes with HCN and NH₃ is a well-established prebiotic amino acid synthesis route (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1854). Montmorillonite is documented in the literature for its role in concentrating organic molecules and protecting fragile aminonitrile intermediates from hydrolytic degradation.

reactions.name: Cross-aldol condensation of formaldehyde and glyceraldehyde to erythrose

conditions.other: Formose-type chain extension; borate complexation of product diol; evaporitic concentration

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde (C₁) undergoes cross-aldol condensation with glyceraldehyde (C₃) to yield erythrose (C₄). The formaldehyde enolate, generated under mild base catalysis, attacks the C₁ carbonyl of glyceraldehyde, extending the carbon chain by one unit.
reactions.reasoning: Cross-aldol condensation between different aldehydes is a standard formose-type reaction (Breslow, 1959). The literature emphasizes that formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde are the primary aldehyde building blocks for tetrose sugar assembly. This C₁+C₃→C₄ route provides an alternative to the C₂+C₂ self-aldol (rxn_010), creating pathway redundancy. Borate stabilization of erythrose follows Ricardo et al. (2004).

reactions.name: Cross-aldol condensation of acetaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to erythrose

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite with NH₃ catalysis at pH ~5.2
conditions.other: Evaporitic pool, wet-dry cycling concentrates reactants; borate complexation stabilizes product

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde (C₂) reacts with glycolaldehyde (C₂) via cross-aldol condensation to form a C₄ hydroxy aldehyde (erythrose/threose). The glycolaldehyde enolate attacks the acetaldehyde carbonyl, yielding a deoxytetrose intermediate that is subsequently hydroxylated or directly produces erythrose depending on the specific aldol regiochemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Cross-aldol condensation between different C₂ aldehydes to form C₄ sugars is a prebiotic sugar pathway variant. The literature specifically notes that 'hydrothermal processing of formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and ammonia during the melting of parent body ice' produces amino acid precursors in carbonaceous chondrites (Pizzarello, 2004; Ehrenfreund et al., 2001). This meteoritic evidence supports the acetaldehyde + glycolaldehyde aldol as a plausible prebiotic route to erythrose.

reactions.name: Erythrose β-dehydration to α-ketoaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mild acid catalysis on montmorillonite surface
conditions.other: Wet-dry cycling drives dehydration; phosphate acts as general acid-base catalyst

reactions.mechanism: Erythrose undergoes β-elimination of water (β-dehydration) to form an α-ketoaldehyde (2-oxo-3-hydroxybutanal). This reaction proceeds via enolization followed by elimination of the β-hydroxyl group. The montmorillonite surface provides Brønsted acid catalysis for the elimination step.
reactions.reasoning: The literature explicitly describes β-dehydration of tetrose sugars to form α-ketoaldehydes as the first step of the prebiotic sugar pathway to amino acid thioesters. The research states: 'The tetrose sugar undergoes β-dehydration to form an α-ketoaldehyde.' This is a crucial activation step that prepares the carbon skeleton for subsequent aminothiol chemistry (Canavelli et al., 2019; Weber, 2001).

reactions.name: Aminothiol rearrangement to threonine thioester

conditions.other: One-pot aqueous system; ΔG°' ≈ -34 kcal/mol (thermodynamically highly favorable); clay adsorption concentrates reactants from dilute solution

reactions.mechanism: The α-ketoaldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form an imine at the keto position. Simultaneously, H₂S attacks the aldehyde to form a hemithioacetal. An intramolecular redox rearrangement of this imine-hemithioacetal adduct transfers the oxidation state from one end of the molecule to the other, producing the activated threonine thioester (S-aminoacyl thioester). This rearrangement is thermodynamically driven (ΔG°' ≈ -34 kcal/mol).
reactions.reasoning: This is the core transformation of the prebiotic sugar pathway, described in detail in the literature: (1) ketoaldehyde + NH₃ + H₂S → imine-hemithioacetal adduct, (2) intramolecular redox rearrangement → activated threonine thioester. The research notes ΔG°' ≈ -34 kcal/mol, indicating a strong thermodynamic drive. The thioester product is pre-disposed to form peptides, addressing the 'activation problem' (Canavelli et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2020). Sulfide minerals provide additional H₂S and catalytic surfaces.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to L-threonine

conditions.other: Mild heating accelerates hydrolysis; concentrated prebiotic pool; nitrile → amide → carboxylic acid

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile undergoes stepwise hydrolysis: first to the amide (threonine amide) via nucleophilic addition of water to the C≡N triple bond, then to the free carboxylic acid (threonine) via further hydrolysis. The reaction is thermodynamically favorable and occurs spontaneously in warm aqueous solution over timescales of days to weeks.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of α-aminonitriles to amino acids is the final step of the classical Strecker synthesis, well-established in prebiotic chemistry (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1854). The Sutherland cyanosulfidic framework identifies the threonine aminonitrile as the penultimate precursor to threonine (Patel et al., 2015). The literature notes that threonine is more thermally stable in alkaline solutions, supporting its survival during hydrolysis at moderate temperatures. The stereochemical outcome (racemic threonine + allothreonine) remains an open challenge.

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine thioester to L-threonine

conditions.other: Prebiotic pool; the thioester is activated and hydrolyzes readily; alternatively participates in peptide formation before hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: The thioester bond (C(=O)-SR) is hydrolyzed by water via nucleophilic acyl substitution. Water attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon, forming a tetrahedral intermediate that collapses to release the thiol leaving group and free threonine. Thioesters are more susceptible to hydrolysis than oxoesters, making this kinetically facile.
reactions.reasoning: Thioester hydrolysis to free amino acid is the terminal step of the prebiotic sugar pathway. The literature explicitly states that the threonine thioester 'can either hydrolyze to free threonine or react with another amino acid to initiate peptide chain growth' (Canavelli et al., 2019). This dual fate is significant for origins of life: the thioester can both generate free threonine and drive peptide bond formation, connecting amino acid synthesis to protometabolism.

reactions.name: Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and glycolaldehyde to glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: Formose-type chain extension; C₁ + C₂ → C₃; borate stabilizes glyceraldehyde against degradation

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde (C₁) reacts with glycolaldehyde (C₂) via base-catalyzed aldol condensation. The glycolaldehyde enolate attacks the formaldehyde carbonyl to form glyceraldehyde (C₃). Borate minerals complex with the vicinal diol of glyceraldehyde, preventing its retro-aldol decomposition and isomerization.
reactions.reasoning: The C₁+C₂→C₃ aldol condensation is a well-known step in the formose reaction (Butlerov, 1861; Breslow, 1959). This provides an alternative non-cyanosulfidic route to glyceraldehyde from hydrothermal formaldehyde, connecting Hydrothermal (formaldehyde from FTT) to Surface (sugar assembly) environments. Borate stabilization demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004).

reactions.name: Formate reduction to formaldehyde on native iron

conditions.other: Serpentinization-derived native iron provides low redox potential; products rapidly removed to cooler zones

reactions.mechanism: Formate is reduced on native iron surfaces through a two-electron reduction. Fe⁰ donates electrons to reduce the carboxylate to the aldehyde oxidation state. The reaction proceeds via a surface-bound formate that is hydrogenated to formaldehyde, with Fe⁰ → Fe²⁺.
reactions.reasoning: Reduction of formate to formaldehyde on native iron surfaces has been explored as a plausible hydrothermal pathway (Preiner et al., 2020). While yields are modest, native iron (Fe⁰) generated during serpentinization provides sufficient reducing power. This reaction connects the well-supported CO₂→formate step (rxn_001) to the critical formaldehyde hub, providing an alternative to the FTT route (rxn_003). The previous attempt's feedback noted this as a weaker link; it is included here with explicit Fe⁰ catalysis and proper citations.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic main pathway (Sutherland)
pathways.description: The primary literature-supported route: volcanic HCN undergoes UV-driven reductive homologation with H₂S and Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox cycling to produce glycolaldehyde, then glyceraldehyde. The threonine aminonitrile forms as an inevitable byproduct of triose chemistry via Strecker reaction of glyceraldehyde + HCN + NH₃. Hydrolysis yields free threonine.

pathways.name: Fischer-Tropsch + Formose + Strecker pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to CO via reverse water-gas shift on magnetite, then CO + H₂ produces formaldehyde via FTT synthesis. Formaldehyde self-condenses via the formose reaction (with borate stabilization) to glycolaldehyde, which undergoes aldol condensation with formaldehyde to glyceraldehyde. The Strecker reaction on glyceraldehyde yields the threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Prebiotic sugar pathway via glycolaldehyde self-aldol
pathways.description: Cyanosulfidic glycolaldehyde undergoes self-aldol condensation to erythrose on montmorillonite with borate stabilization. Erythrose is dehydrated to α-ketoaldehyde, which reacts with NH₃ and H₂S via the aminothiol rearrangement to produce the activated threonine thioester. Hydrolysis yields free threonine.

pathways.name: Prebiotic sugar pathway via cross-aldol (formaldehyde + glyceraldehyde)
pathways.description: Glyceraldehyde from the cyanosulfidic cascade undergoes cross-aldol condensation with hydrothermal formaldehyde to produce erythrose. The erythrose then enters the prebiotic sugar pathway via β-dehydration and aminothiol rearrangement to the threonine thioester.

pathways.name: Acetaldehyde cross-aldol to erythrose pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch synthesis produces acetaldehyde. In surface pools, acetaldehyde undergoes cross-aldol condensation with cyanosulfidic glycolaldehyde on montmorillonite to form erythrose. The prebiotic sugar pathway then converts erythrose to threonine thioester and then free threonine.

pathways.name: Formate → Formaldehyde → Formose → Strecker pathway
pathways.description: CO₂ is reduced to formate on greigite, formate is further reduced to formaldehyde on native iron, formaldehyde enters the formose reaction to produce glycolaldehyde, which is extended to glyceraldehyde via aldol, and the Strecker reaction yields threonine aminonitrile.

pathways.name: Mixed formose + prebiotic sugar pathway
pathways.description: Formaldehyde from hydrothermal FTT undergoes formose self-condensation to glycolaldehyde. Glycolaldehyde self-condenses to erythrose via aldol on montmorillonite. Erythrose enters the prebiotic sugar pathway (dehydration → aminothiol → thioester → threonine).

pathways.name: Dual cyanosulfidic entry with dihydroxyacetone branch
pathways.description: Glyceraldehyde from the cyanosulfidic cascade isomerizes to dihydroxyacetone (59% yield). The DHA/glyceraldehyde equilibrium provides a reservoir of C₃ intermediates. From the remaining glyceraldehyde pool, the Strecker pathway proceeds to threonine aminonitrile and then to threonine. The DHA branch represents a competing sink that modulates threonine yield.

pathways.name: Meteoritic/parent body analog: acetaldehyde + formaldehyde to erythrose
pathways.description: Analogous to parent body processing in carbonaceous chondrites. FTT acetaldehyde and FTT formaldehyde both reach surface environments. Formaldehyde condenses to glycolaldehyde via formose, then glycolaldehyde + acetaldehyde cross-aldol produces erythrose. The prebiotic sugar pathway completes the route to threonine.

pathways.name: Fully cyanosulfidic thioester pathway
pathways.description: HCN undergoes complete cyanosulfidic reductive homologation to glycolaldehyde and then glyceraldehyde. Glyceraldehyde + formaldehyde (from FTT) cross-aldol produces erythrose. Erythrose enters the prebiotic sugar pathway via dehydration and aminothiol rearrangement to give the threonine thioester. This represents the maximal integration of cyanosulfidic and sugar chemistry.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the core Sutherland cyanosulfidic route and is well-supported by literature. The reductive homologation steps (rxn_006, rxn_007) are experimentally validated. However, rxn_005 (NH3 + CO → HCN) is a non-standard route for HCN generation; volcanic HCN typically forms from N2 + CH4 or NH3 + CH2O at extreme temperatures. More critically, rxn_011 oversimplifies the formation of threonine aminonitrile from glyceraldehyde; a direct Strecker on glyceraldehyde would yield a serine-related derivative, not threonine. The literature describes the threonine aminonitrile as an 'inevitable byproduct' of triose chemistry, implying a more complex network of intermediates rather than a single step. Stereochemical selectivity for L-threonine is also not addressed.
suggestions: Replace rxn_005 with a more standard HCN formation route (e.g., NH3 + CH2O → HCN or N2 + CH4 spark discharge)
Decompose rxn_011 into sub-steps showing the actual triose-derived rearrangement that yields 2-amino-3-hydroxybutanenitrile rather than a direct Strecker on glyceraldehyde
Address stereoselectivity explicitly, perhaps via chiral mineral surface catalysis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-constructed pathway combining FTT synthesis, formose chemistry, and Strecker synthesis. The RWGS reaction (rxn_002) is thermodynamically valid at hydrothermal temperatures. Formaldehyde from FTT (rxn_003) has lower yields due to formaldehyde instability, but is plausible. The formose self-condensation (rxn_008) and aldol extension (rxn_018) are classic reactions with borate mineral stabilization referenced. Same issue with rxn_011 as in P1.
suggestions: Specify borate mineral stabilization explicitly for the formose steps to prevent sugar degradation
Address the low yield issue of formaldehyde from FTT synthesis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This pathway represents the Prebiotic Sugar Pathway excellently, closely matching literature descriptions. The glycolaldehyde self-aldol to erythrose (rxn_010) is chemically plausible, though stereoselectivity to erythrose over threose is not guaranteed. The β-dehydration of erythrose to α-ketoaldehyde (rxn_014) followed by the aminothiol rearrangement (rxn_015) to form the activated thioester is well-supported by literature citing ΔG°' ≈ -34 kcal/mol. Thioester hydrolysis (rxn_017) is straightforward. This is arguably the most chemically coherent pathway in the network.
suggestions: Specify the pH conditions for the aldol condensation (literature suggests pH ~5.2 with amino acid catalysis)
Elaborate on diastereoselectivity of the erythrose formation step

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good alternative route through formaldehyde + glyceraldehyde cross-aldol. The cross-aldol condensation (rxn_012) to produce erythrose is chemically plausible, though typically produces a mixture of tetrose stereoisomers. The pathway combines hydrothermal C1 generation with surface sugar chemistry effectively.
suggestions: Address the mixture of tetrose products (erythrose vs threose) from cross-aldol

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Longer pathway that introduces acetaldehyde from FTT. The acetaldehyde + glycolaldehyde cross-aldol (rxn_013) is plausible but less well-documented prebiotically than formaldehyde-based aldols. FTT production of acetaldehyde (rxn_004) has low expected yields. The pathway is viable but less efficient than alternatives.
suggestions: Provide evidence for acetaldehyde cross-aldol selectivity
Consider whether acetaldehyde + glycolaldehyde truly gives erythrose or a different C4 product

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alternative hydrothermal entry through formate rather than CO, adding pathway redundancy. Formate reduction on native iron (rxn_019) is experimentally demonstrated. The remainder follows the formose + Strecker route with the same rxn_011 concern.
suggestions: Same improvement needed for rxn_011 as in P1

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Combines formose chemistry entry with the prebiotic sugar pathway exit. Good hybrid pathway demonstrating the network's interconnectedness. All individual steps are chemically reasonable.
suggestions: Ensure the formose glycolaldehyde is produced in sufficient quantities for subsequent self-aldol

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The DHA branch (rxn_009) is chemically well-supported (59% yield in literature) but DHA itself is not directly utilized to produce threonine in this network. DHA appears as a dead-end intermediate (mol_013 does not feed into any subsequent reaction leading to threonine). The pathway's inclusion of the isomerization is interesting for completeness but does not enhance target achievement.
suggestions: Connect DHA to the erythrose pathway via an aldol condensation with formaldehyde
Otherwise remove the DHA branch from threonine-targeting pathways

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This long pathway models parent body processing analogous to meteoritic chemistry. While the concept is interesting and aligns with literature on Murchison meteorite amino acids, the 8-step length introduces cumulative yield losses. The acetaldehyde + glycolaldehyde cross-aldol step remains the weakest link. The pathway provides important contextual support for extraterrestrial formation but is less efficient than terrestrial alternatives.
suggestions: Consider adding aqueous alteration conditions specific to parent body environments
Provide yield estimates for cumulative 8-step pathway

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Excellent pathway that combines the cyanosulfidic entry (generating C2, C3 building blocks) with the prebiotic sugar pathway exit (thioester formation). This bridges both major literature frameworks into a single coherent route. The cyanosulfidic generation of glyceraldehyde feeds directly into erythrose formation, and the thioester activation provides a prebiotically relevant activated amino acid. The rxn_005 HCN generation concern persists.
suggestions: Strengthen the HCN formation step with a more established mechanism

evaluation.network_feedback: This is an excellent synthesis network for prebiotic L-threonine that demonstrates strong alignment with current literature, particularly the Sutherland cyanosulfidic protometabolism and the Prebiotic Sugar Pathway. The network topology is impressive with 18 molecules, 19 reactions, 4 well-connected hub molecules, and 5 convergence points. The dual convergence on two activated intermediates (threonine aminonitrile and threonine thioester) provides robust redundancy. Environmental progression from hydrothermal (C1 generation on greigite/magnetite) through surface (UV photochemistry, formose, Strecker, aldol) to biochemical (hydrolysis) is well-justified and clearly structured. Key strengths: (1) Excellent literature alignment with both Sutherland and prebiotic sugar pathway frameworks, (2) multiple redundant routes providing robustness, (3) proper mineral catalyst specification throughout, (4) hub molecules are chemically justified and well-connected. Key weaknesses: (1) rxn_011 oversimplifies the mechanism for threonine aminonitrile formation from glyceraldehyde—a direct Strecker would give a serine derivative; the actual pathway involves more complex triose rearrangements, (2) rxn_005 uses a non-standard HCN synthesis route, (3) stereoselectivity for L-threonine is unaddressed throughout—prebiotic Strecker and aldol reactions typically yield racemic mixtures, (4) DHA (mol_013) is a dead-end node not connected to the target, (5) thermal stability constraints on threonine (degradation above 120°C) are not incorporated into pathway design. Despite these issues, the network successfully achieves the target through multiple chemically plausible routes and represents a high-quality systems chemistry approach to prebiotic threonine synthesis.
